1:
"I charge thee therefore before God, and the Lord Jesus Christ, who shall judge the quick and the dead at his appearing and his kingdom;" The witnesses note the solemn charge before God and Christ the Judge. On "I charge thee therefore before God, and the Lord Jesus Christ," the grammatical voices note the solemnity. Barnes and Robertson note the weight of the adjuration: "I charge (diamartyromai — solemnly testify, adjure) thee before (in the sight and presence of) God, and the Lord Jesus Christ" — Paul lays his charge on Timothy in the most solemn manner, as in the very presence of God and Christ.

On "who shall judge the quick and the dead," the witnesses note the Judge. Barnes and Calvin note the description of Christ: "who shall judge the quick (the living) and the dead" — Christ the universal Judge of all, living and dead; the charge is given before the One to whom Timothy must finally answer.

On "at his appearing and his kingdom," the eastern Chrysostom and grammatical-historical note the day: "at (and by) his appearing (epiphaneia — his glorious second coming) and his kingdom" — the charge is reinforced by the prospect of Christ's appearing and the kingdom he will then establish; the coming judgment gives the charge its weight. The verse displays the solemn charge before God and Christ the Judge.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the weight — "I charge (solemnly adjure) thee before (in the presence of) God and the Lord Jesus Christ," laid on Timothy in the most solemn manner (Barnes, Robertson); "who shall judge the quick (living) and the dead," Christ the universal Judge before whom Timothy must answer (Barnes, Calvin); "at his appearing and his kingdom," reinforced by the prospect of Christ's coming and kingdom (Chrysostom, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the charge. The verse marks the adjuration: I charge thee. The verse displays the witnesses: God and Christ. The verse displays the Judge: of quick and dead. The verse displays the day: his appearing. The charge displays Timothy adjured before the coming Judge. The verse displays the charging: solemn. The verse displays the judgment: universal. The verse displays the appearing: glorious. The solemn charge before God and Christ the Judge displays the gravity with which Paul lays his final command: he adjures Timothy in the presence of God and the Lord Jesus Christ — who will judge the living and the dead at his appearing and kingdom — to discharge his ministry. The verse displays the solemn adjuration, issuing in the charge to preach the word (v.2).
2:
"Preach the word; be instant in season, out of season; reprove, rebuke, exhort with all longsuffering and doctrine." The witnesses note the great charge to preach the word in season and out of season. On "preach the word," the grammatical voices note the central command. Barnes and Robertson note the heart of the charge: "preach (keryxon — herald, proclaim as a herald) the word (the gospel, the message of God)" — the supreme duty of the minister is to proclaim the word of God; this is his commission.

On "be instant in season, out of season," the witnesses note the constancy. Barnes and Calvin note the urgency: "be instant (epistethi — be ready, stand by, be urgent, persist) in season, out of season (when convenient and when inconvenient, at all times)" — the minister is to be ever ready to preach, whether the occasion seems favourable or not; opportune and inopportune alike.

On "reprove, rebuke, exhort with all longsuffering and doctrine," the puritan Henry notes the threefold work and its spirit: "reprove (convict, refute error), rebuke (censure sin), exhort (encourage to duty)" — and all "with all longsuffering (patience) and doctrine (teaching)" — the varied work of preaching done patiently and with sound instruction. The verse displays the great charge to preach the word.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the central command — "preach (herald, proclaim) the word (of God)," the minister's supreme duty (Barnes, Robertson); "be instant (ready, urgent) in season, out of season," ever ready whether the occasion is favourable or not (Barnes, Calvin); "reprove (refute error), rebuke (censure sin), exhort (encourage)... with all longsuffering and doctrine," the varied work done patiently with sound teaching (Henry). The verse displays the charge to preach. The verse marks the command: preach the word. The verse displays the constancy: in season, out of season. The verse displays the work: reprove, rebuke, exhort. The verse displays the spirit: longsuffering and doctrine. The charge displays Timothy ever proclaiming the word. The verse displays the preaching: heralded. The verse displays the readiness: always. The verse displays the patience: all longsuffering. The great charge to preach the word in season and out of season displays the heart of Paul's final command: Timothy is to herald the word of God, ever ready whether convenient or not — reproving, rebuking, and exhorting, with all patience and sound teaching. The verse displays the charge to preach, urgent because of coming apostasy (vv.3-4).
3:
"For the time will come when they will not endure sound doctrine; but after their own lusts shall they heap to themselves teachers, having itching ears;" The witnesses note the coming time when men will not endure sound doctrine. On "the time will come when they will not endure sound doctrine," the grammatical voices note the prediction. Barnes and Robertson note the urgency behind the charge to preach: "the time will come when they will not endure (bear, tolerate) sound (healthful, wholesome) doctrine" — a season is coming when men will refuse the wholesome teaching of the gospel; this is why Timothy must preach faithfully now.

On "after their own lusts shall they heap to themselves teachers," the witnesses note the self-chosen teachers. Barnes and Calvin note the perversity: "after their own lusts (desires) shall they heap (accumulate, pile up) to themselves teachers" — instead of submitting to sound teaching, they will gather a multitude of teachers who flatter their desires; they choose teachers to suit their lusts.

On "having itching ears," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the figure: "having itching ears (ears that crave novelty and flattery, needing to be tickled, never satisfied)" — restless ears that itch for new and pleasing things, demanding teachers who will gratify rather than correct them. The verse displays the coming time when men will not endure sound doctrine.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the prediction — "the time will come when they will not endure (tolerate) sound (wholesome) doctrine," men refusing the gospel's teaching, the urgency behind the charge (Barnes, Robertson); "after their own lusts shall they heap (pile up) to themselves teachers," gathering flattering teachers to suit their desires (Barnes, Calvin); "having itching ears," restless ears craving novelty and flattery (Wesley). The verse displays the refusal of sound doctrine. The verse marks the time: it will come. The verse displays the refusal: not endure sound doctrine. The verse displays the self-choosing: heap up teachers. The verse displays the figure: itching ears. The prediction displays men choosing flatterers over truth. The verse displays the doctrine: refused. The verse displays the teachers: heaped. The verse displays the ears: itching. The coming time when men will not endure sound doctrine displays the urgency behind Paul's charge: a season is coming when men, refusing wholesome teaching, will gather to themselves a crowd of teachers to flatter their own desires — having itching ears that crave novelty. The verse displays the apostasy of itching ears, turning from truth to fables (v.4).
4:
"And they shall turn away their ears from the truth, and shall be turned unto fables." The brief verse notes the turning from truth to fables. The grammatical voices note the double turning. Barnes and Robertson note the result of itching ears: "they shall turn away (apostrepsousin — turn aside, avert) their ears from the truth, and shall be turned (ektrapesontai — be turned aside, diverted) unto fables (mythous — myths, fictions, idle tales)" — they reject the truth and embrace fiction; a turning away from the real and toward the false.

On "turn away their ears from the truth," the witnesses note the rejection. Barnes and Calvin note the willful aversion: they "turn away their ears from the truth" — deliberately refusing to hear the truth that corrects them; the ear that itches for flattery shuts itself against truth.

On "turned unto fables," the grammatical-historical note the substitute: they "shall be turned unto fables" — to myths, idle speculations, and false teachings; having rejected the truth, they fall into fiction; the alternative to truth is not neutrality but error. The verse displays the turning from truth to fables.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the result — "they shall turn away (avert) their ears from the truth, and shall be turned (diverted) unto fables (myths, fictions)," rejecting truth and embracing fiction (Barnes, Robertson); the willful aversion, deliberately refusing the truth that corrects (Barnes, Calvin); "turned unto fables," falling into myth and error, the alternative to truth being error not neutrality (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the turning. The verse marks the rejection: turn from the truth. The verse displays the substitute: turned unto fables. The verse displays the willfulness: deliberate aversion. The verse displays the alternative: error not neutrality. The turning displays men forsaking truth for fiction. The verse displays the truth: rejected. The verse displays the fables: embraced. The verse displays the turning: double. The turning from truth to fables displays the end of itching ears: men will deliberately avert their ears from the truth and be turned aside to myths and idle fictions — for having rejected the truth, they fall not into neutrality but into error. The verse displays the apostasy to fables, against which Timothy must watch and endure (v.5).
5:
"But watch thou in all things, endure afflictions, do the work of an evangelist, make full proof of thy ministry." The witnesses note the fourfold charge: watch, endure, evangelize, fulfil the ministry. On "watch thou in all things," the grammatical voices note the vigilance. Barnes and Robertson note the first charge over against the coming apostasy: "watch (nephe — be sober, be vigilant, keep clear-headed) thou in all things" — Timothy is to be watchful and self-possessed in everything, alert against error and danger.

On "endure afflictions, do the work of an evangelist," the witnesses note the next two. Barnes and Calvin note: "endure afflictions (kakopatheson — suffer hardship, bear suffering)" — bearing the sufferings of the ministry; and "do the work of an evangelist (a preacher of the gospel, a bringer of good news)" — fulfilling the evangelist's task of proclaiming Christ.

On "make full proof of thy ministry," the puritan Henry notes the climax: "make full proof of (plerophoreson — fully discharge, completely fulfil, carry out to the full) thy ministry" — Timothy is to perform his ministry completely, leaving nothing of it undone; the full and faithful discharge of his office. The verse displays the fourfold charge: watch, endure, evangelize, fulfil the ministry.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the fourfold charge — "watch (be sober, vigilant) thou in all things," watchful and alert against error (Barnes, Robertson); "endure afflictions (suffer hardship)" and "do the work of an evangelist (proclaim the gospel)," bearing suffering and fulfilling the evangelist's task (Barnes, Calvin); "make full proof of (fully discharge) thy ministry," performing it completely (Henry). The verse displays the fourfold charge. The verse marks the vigilance: watch in all things. The verse displays the endurance: endure afflictions. The verse displays the work: of an evangelist. The verse displays the climax: fulfil thy ministry. The charge displays Timothy watchful, enduring, and fully faithful. The verse displays the watching: sober. The verse displays the enduring: hardship. The verse displays the ministry: fulfilled. The fourfold charge — watch, endure, evangelize, fulfil the ministry — displays the summary of Paul's command to Timothy: he is to be vigilant in all things, bear the sufferings of his office, do the work of an evangelist, and fully discharge his whole ministry. The verse displays the charge to faithfulness, sealed by Paul's own finished course (vv.6-8).
6:
"For I am now ready to be offered, and the time of my departure is at hand." The witnesses note Paul ready to be offered, his departure at hand. On "I am now ready to be offered," the grammatical voices note the sacrifice. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's serene view of his approaching death: "I am now ready to be offered (spendomai — being poured out as a drink-offering, a libation)" — he sees his coming martyrdom as a sacrifice, his lifeblood poured out as a libation upon the altar; death as an offering to God.

On "the time of my departure is at hand," the witnesses note the imminence. Barnes and Calvin note the figure: "the time of my departure (analysis — loosing, as a ship from its moorings or the striking of a tent) is at hand (has come, is present)" — Paul's death is imminent; he speaks of it not as an end but as a departure, a loosing to set sail or a breaking of camp.

On the tone, the puritan Henry notes the serenity: the aged apostle faces death with calm, even joy — seeing it as a sacrifice acceptable to God and a departure to be with Christ; the manner of his facing death is itself part of his final example. The verse displays Paul ready to be offered, his departure at hand.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's serene view — "I am now ready to be offered (poured out as a drink-offering)," his martyrdom a sacrifice, his lifeblood a libation (Barnes, Robertson); "the time of my departure (loosing, as a ship or tent) is at hand," his death imminent, a departure not an end (Barnes, Calvin); the serenity, facing death with calm and joy as sacrifice and departure (Henry). The verse displays his approaching death. The verse marks the offering: poured out. The verse displays the departure: at hand. The verse displays the figures: libation, loosing. The verse displays the tone: serene. The view displays Paul facing death as sacrifice and departure. The verse displays the offering: ready. The verse displays the departure: imminent. The verse displays the serenity: calm. Paul ready to be offered, his departure at hand, displays the serenity of the apostle facing martyrdom: he sees his coming death as a sacrifice — his lifeblood poured out as a drink-offering — and as a departure, a loosing to set sail; calmly he faces the end. The verse displays the apostle's approaching death, looking back on a finished course (v.7).
7:
"I have fought a good fight, I have finished my course, I have kept the faith:" The witnesses note Paul's threefold triumphant review of his life. On "I have fought a good fight," the grammatical voices note the contest. Barnes and Robertson note the first figure, from the games or warfare: "I have fought (egonismai — contended, striven) a good fight (the good contest, agon)" — Paul looks back on his whole life as a noble contest well fought, whether the wrestling-match, the boxing, or the spiritual warfare.

On "I have finished my course," the witnesses note the race. Barnes and Calvin note the second figure, from the foot-race: "I have finished (teteleka — completed, run to the end) my course (dromon — the race-course, the appointed track)" — he has run the race of his life and ministry to its appointed end; the course is complete.

On "I have kept the faith," the puritan Henry notes the third: "I have kept (tetereka — guarded, preserved) the faith" — whether the body of Christian truth (the deposit, kept pure) or his own fidelity and pledged loyalty (kept unbroken, as a soldier keeps his oath); he has held fast the faith to the end. The verse displays Paul's threefold triumphant review of his life.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the threefold review — "I have fought (contended) a good fight (noble contest)," his life a contest well fought (Barnes, Robertson); "I have finished (run to the end) my course (the appointed race)," the race of his life complete (Barnes, Calvin); "I have kept (guarded) the faith," whether the body of truth kept pure or his own fidelity kept unbroken (Henry). The verse displays the triumphant review. The verse marks the fight: fought well. The verse displays the course: finished. The verse displays the faith: kept. The verse displays the triumph: complete. The review displays Paul looking back on a faithful life. The verse displays the fighting: good. The verse displays the finishing: of the course. The verse displays the keeping: of the faith. Paul's threefold triumphant review of his life displays the serene confidence of the dying apostle: he has fought the good fight, finished his race, and kept the faith — his contest nobly waged, his course run to the end, and the faith held fast to the last. The verse displays the finished course, crowned with a crown of righteousness (v.8).
8:
"Henceforth there is laid up for me a crown of righteousness, which the Lord, the righteous judge, shall give me at that day: and not to me only, but unto all them also that love his appearing." The witnesses note the crown of righteousness laid up for Paul and all who love Christ's appearing. On "henceforth there is laid up for me a crown of righteousness," the grammatical voices note the reward. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's certain hope: "henceforth (for the rest, from now on) there is laid up (reserved, stored up) for me a crown (stephanos — the victor's wreath) of righteousness" — the reward awaiting him, a crown that is the gift and fruit of righteousness, reserved in heaven.

On "which the Lord, the righteous judge, shall give me at that day," the witnesses note the Giver and the day. Barnes and Calvin note: "the Lord, the righteous judge (who judges justly), shall give (award) me at that day (the day of his appearing and judgment)" — Christ the just Judge will award the crown; the reward is sure because the Judge is righteous; given not now but at that day.

On "not to me only, but unto all them also that love his appearing," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the breadth: the crown is "not to me only, but unto all them also that love (have loved and long for) his appearing" — not Paul's alone but the portion of all who love and look for Christ's return; the hope belongs to every faithful believer. The verse displays the crown of righteousness laid up for Paul and all who love Christ's appearing.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's hope — "henceforth there is laid up (reserved) for me a crown (victor's wreath) of righteousness," the reward awaiting him, the fruit of righteousness (Barnes, Robertson); "the Lord, the righteous judge, shall give me at that day," Christ the just Judge awarding it at his appearing, sure because the Judge is righteous (Barnes, Calvin); "not to me only, but unto all them also that love his appearing," the portion of all who love and look for Christ's return (Wesley). The verse displays the crown. The verse marks the reward: a crown of righteousness. The verse displays the Giver: the righteous Judge. The verse displays the day: that day. The verse displays the breadth: all who love his appearing. The crown displays the reward laid up for the faithful. The verse displays the crown: reserved. The verse displays the Judge: righteous. The verse displays the lovers: of his appearing. The crown of righteousness laid up for Paul and all who love Christ's appearing displays the certain hope that crowns his finished course: a victor's wreath is reserved for him, which the righteous Judge will award at that day — and not to Paul only, but to all who love and long for Christ's appearing. The verse displays the believer's crown, before Paul's personal requests (vv.9-13).
9:
"Do thy diligence to come shortly unto me:" The brief verse notes Paul's urgent request that Timothy come quickly. The grammatical voices note the appeal. Barnes and Robertson note the sudden, personal turn from the heights of the crown to a tender request: "do thy diligence (spoudason — make haste, give diligence, do your best) to come shortly (quickly, soon) unto me" — the aged, lonely apostle urgently longs for Timothy's company before his death.

On "come shortly unto me," the eastern Chrysostom notes the loneliness. Chrysostom and Henry note the pathos: Paul, facing death and largely forsaken (vv.10-11, 16), yearns for his beloved son to come; the urgency ("shortly," and again "before winter," v.21) reflects both his loneliness and the shortness of the time left.

On the request, the grammatical-historical note the human tenderness: even the great apostle, serene before martyrdom, feels the natural longing for a friend's presence in his last days; the request reveals the warmth of his heart. The verse displays Paul's urgent request that Timothy come quickly.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the personal turn — "do thy diligence (make haste, do your best) to come shortly (quickly) unto me," the lonely apostle urgently longing for Timothy before his death (Barnes, Robertson); the pathos, Paul facing death and largely forsaken yearning for his son, the urgency reflecting his loneliness and the short time (Chrysostom, Henry); even the great apostle feeling the natural longing for a friend (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the urgent request. The verse marks the appeal: do thy diligence. The verse displays the haste: come shortly. The verse displays the loneliness: forsaken. The verse displays the tenderness: human longing. The request displays Paul yearning for Timothy's company. The verse displays the diligence: urged. The verse displays the coming: soon. The verse displays the longing: for his son. Paul's urgent request that Timothy come quickly displays the human tenderness of the dying apostle: turning from the crown to a personal plea, he urges his beloved son to make haste and come to him soon — for he is lonely, largely forsaken, and his time is short. The verse displays the apostle's longing, deepened by the desertion of Demas (v.10).
10:
"For Demas hath forsaken me, having loved this present world, and is departed unto Thessalonica; Crescens to Galatia, Titus unto Dalmatia." The witnesses note the desertion of Demas and the dispersal of Paul's companions. On "Demas hath forsaken me, having loved this present world," the grammatical voices note the defection. Barnes and Robertson note the sad report: "Demas (once Paul's fellow-labourer, Colossians 4:14; Philemon 24) hath forsaken (abandoned, deserted) me, having loved this present world (the present age, its ease and safety)" — Demas, from love of the world and fear of danger, abandoned the imprisoned apostle.

On "having loved this present world," the witnesses note the cause. Barnes and Calvin note the root of his desertion: not open apostasy from the faith necessarily, but love of "this present world" — its comforts, safety, or gain — which drew him away from Paul in his peril; worldliness the cause of his unfaithfulness.

On "Crescens to Galatia, Titus unto Dalmatia," the grammatical-historical note the others: "Crescens (gone) to Galatia, Titus unto Dalmatia" — these two had left, not necessarily in desertion like Demas, but sent or gone on ministry to other regions; whatever the reason, Paul was left increasingly alone. The verse displays the desertion of Demas and the dispersal of Paul's companions.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sad report — "Demas (once a fellow-labourer) hath forsaken (deserted) me, having loved this present world," abandoning the imprisoned apostle from love of the world (Barnes, Robertson); the cause being love of "this present world," its comforts and safety, drawing him from Paul in peril (Barnes, Calvin); "Crescens to Galatia, Titus unto Dalmatia," the others gone, perhaps on ministry, Paul left increasingly alone (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the desertion. The verse marks the defection: Demas forsook me. The verse displays the cause: loved this world. The verse displays the dispersal: Crescens, Titus gone. The verse displays the loneliness: increasing. The desertion displays Paul left alone by departed companions. The verse displays the Demas: forsaking. The verse displays the world: loved. The verse displays the companions: dispersed. The desertion of Demas and the dispersal of Paul's companions display the loneliness of the apostle's last days: Demas, from love of this present world, forsook him; Crescens and Titus had gone to other regions — so that Paul was left increasingly alone. The verse displays the scattering of his friends, leaving only Luke (v.11).
11:
"Only Luke is with me. Take Mark, and bring him with thee: for he is profitable to me for the ministry." The witnesses note Luke alone with Paul, and the request for the restored Mark. On "only Luke is with me," the grammatical voices note the faithful companion. Barnes and Robertson note the touching note: "only Luke (the beloved physician, the evangelist) is with me" — of all his companions, only Luke remained at Paul's side in his imprisonment; the faithful friend who stayed to the end.

On "take Mark, and bring him with thee," the witnesses note the restored Mark. Barnes and Calvin note the request: "take Mark (John Mark), and bring him with thee" — the same Mark who had once departed from Paul, causing the sharp contention with Barnabas (Acts 15:37-39), now fully reconciled and desired; a beautiful token of restored fellowship.

On "for he is profitable to me for the ministry," the puritan Henry notes the value: Mark "is profitable (useful, serviceable) to me for the ministry (for service, ministering help)" — the once-rejected Mark is now valued as useful for the work; the restoration is complete, Paul desiring his help. The verse displays Luke alone with Paul, and the request for the restored Mark.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the faithful companion — "only Luke (the beloved physician) is with me," only Luke remaining at Paul's side, faithful to the end (Barnes, Robertson); "take Mark (John Mark), and bring him with thee," the once-departed Mark now reconciled and desired, restored fellowship (Acts 15, Barnes, Calvin); "for he is profitable (useful) to me for the ministry," the once-rejected Mark now valued for service (Henry). The verse displays Luke and Mark. The verse marks the companion: only Luke. The verse displays the request: bring Mark. The verse displays the restoration: reconciled. The verse displays the value: profitable for ministry. The note displays Paul's faithful and restored friends. The verse displays the Luke: faithful. The verse displays the Mark: restored. The verse displays the profit: for ministry. Luke alone with Paul, and the request for the restored Mark, display both the loneliness and the grace of his last days: only the faithful Luke remained at his side, and he asks for Mark — once the cause of a sharp parting, now reconciled and valued as profitable for the ministry. The verse displays the faithful and restored, with Tychicus sent away (v.12).
12:
"And Tychicus have I sent to Ephesus." The brief verse notes Tychicus sent to Ephesus. The grammatical voices note the dispatch. Barnes and Robertson note the brief item: "and Tychicus (Paul's trusted companion and frequent messenger, Ephesians 6:21; Colossians 4:7) have I sent to Ephesus" — Tychicus, another of Paul's faithful helpers, had been sent on an errand to Ephesus, perhaps to relieve Timothy so that he might come to Paul.

On the sending, the witnesses note the possible purpose. Barnes and Ellicott note that Tychicus may have been sent to take Timothy's place at Ephesus (where Timothy was, 1 Timothy 1:3), freeing him to travel to Rome; the dispatch fits Paul's request that Timothy come (v.9).

On the trusted messenger, the grammatical-historical note Tychicus's record: he was the bearer of the Ephesian and Colossian letters and a "beloved brother and faithful minister" (Colossians 4:7); another of the faithful, now sent on service. The verse displays Tychicus sent to Ephesus.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the brief item — "Tychicus (Paul's trusted messenger) have I sent to Ephesus," another faithful helper sent on an errand (Barnes, Robertson); possibly to take Timothy's place at Ephesus, freeing him to come to Rome (v.9, Barnes, Ellicott); Tychicus a beloved brother and faithful minister, bearer of earlier letters (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dispatch. The verse marks the messenger: Tychicus. The verse displays the errand: to Ephesus. The verse displays the purpose: perhaps to free Timothy. The verse displays the record: faithful minister. The dispatch displays Paul sending his trusted helper. The verse displays the Tychicus: sent. The verse displays the Ephesus: his errand. The verse displays the faithfulness: known. Tychicus sent to Ephesus displays the disposition of Paul's faithful helpers in his last days: Tychicus, his trusted messenger and beloved brother, had been sent on an errand to Ephesus — perhaps to relieve Timothy there, freeing him to travel to Paul. The verse displays the sent messenger, amid Paul's requests for his cloak and books (v.13). What is quietly moving in this brief notice is the way it fits into the larger picture of the last days of the apostle, for it shows him, even from a Roman dungeon and under the shadow of execution, still ordering the affairs of the churches and disposing of his fellow-workers for their good; the very sending of so trusted a helper as Tychicus to Ephesus, while it left Paul the poorer in company, was likely a deliberate kindness meant to release Timothy from his charge there so that the beloved son might hasten to his father before the end.
13:
"The cloke that I left at Troas with Carpus, when thou comest, bring with thee, and the books, but especially the parchments." The witnesses note Paul's request for his cloak, books, and parchments. On "the cloke that I left at Troas with Carpus," the grammatical voices note the garment. Barnes and Robertson note the homely, touching request: "the cloke (phelones — a heavy outer travelling-cloak, for warmth against the cold) that I left at Troas with Carpus (a host or friend there), when thou comest, bring with thee" — the aged prisoner, facing a cold dungeon and winter (v.21), asks for his warm cloak.

On "the books, but especially the parchments," the witnesses note the writings. Barnes and Calvin note the request for his writings: "the books (biblia — papyrus rolls), but especially the parchments (membranas — the vellum documents, more durable and valuable)" — perhaps copies of the Scriptures, his own notes, or important documents; even facing death, Paul wants his books, especially the precious parchments.

On the touching detail, the puritan Henry notes the humanity: these small, practical requests — a cloak against the cold, books to read — reveal the apostle's human condition in prison and his continued love of study even at the end; the great man near death still cares for his cloak and his books. The verse displays Paul's request for his cloak, books, and parchments.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the homely request — "the cloke (heavy travelling-cloak for warmth) left at Troas with Carpus... bring," the aged prisoner asking for his warm cloak against the cold (Barnes, Robertson); "the books (papyrus rolls), but especially the parchments (vellum, more valuable)," perhaps Scriptures or notes, wanted even facing death (Barnes, Calvin); the touching humanity, the great man still caring for cloak and books (Henry). The verse displays the request. The verse marks the cloak: for warmth. The verse displays the books: papyrus. The verse displays the parchments: especially. The verse displays the humanity: practical needs. The request displays Paul's human condition and love of study. The verse displays the cloak: left at Troas. The verse displays the books: requested. The verse displays the parchments: prized. Paul's request for his cloak, books, and parchments displays the touching humanity of the dying apostle: facing a cold dungeon and winter, he asks Timothy to bring the warm cloak he left at Troas, and his books — especially the precious parchments — for even at the end he loves to study. The verse displays the apostle's human needs, amid a warning against Alexander (vv.14-15).
14:
"Alexander the coppersmith did me much evil: the Lord reward him according to his works:" The witnesses note Alexander the coppersmith's harm and Paul's committal of him to God. On "Alexander the coppersmith did me much evil," the grammatical voices note the adversary. Barnes and Robertson note the warning about a personal enemy: "Alexander the coppersmith (a metalworker, perhaps the Alexander of 1 Timothy 1:20 or Acts 19:33) did me much evil (showed me much ill, did me many injuries)" — a determined opponent who had done Paul great harm, perhaps testifying against him or stirring up opposition.

On "the Lord reward him according to his works," interpreters note the expression. Barnes and Calvin note the phrase: "the Lord reward (apodoie — render, repay) him according to his works" — most take this not as a vindictive curse but as a committal of the matter to God's just judgment; some manuscripts read "will reward" (a statement, not a wish), leaving vengeance to God; Paul does not avenge himself but leaves Alexander to the righteous Judge (cf. Romans 12:19).

On the spirit, the puritan Henry notes the restraint: Paul, though wronged, does not retaliate but commits the wrong to God; the words express not personal malice but a solemn leaving of judgment to the Lord. The verse displays Alexander's harm and Paul's committal of him to God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the adversary — "Alexander the coppersmith did me much evil (many injuries)," a determined opponent who harmed Paul greatly (Barnes, Robertson); "the Lord reward (repay) him according to his works," most taking it not as vindictive but a committal to God's just judgment (some read "will reward," a statement), leaving vengeance to God (Romans 12:19, Barnes, Calvin); Paul not retaliating but committing the wrong to God (Henry). The verse displays the adversary committed to God. The verse marks the harm: much evil. The verse displays the opponent: Alexander. The verse displays the committal: the Lord reward him. The verse displays the spirit: vengeance left to God. The note displays Paul leaving his enemy to the righteous Judge. The verse displays the evil: done. The verse displays the reward: God's. The verse displays the restraint: no retaliation. Alexander the coppersmith's harm and Paul's committal of him to God display the apostle's response to a personal enemy: Alexander had done him much evil, yet Paul does not avenge himself but leaves him to the Lord, who will repay according to his works. The verse displays the wrong left to God, with a warning to Timothy (v.15).
15:
"Of whom be thou ware also; for he hath greatly withstood our words." The brief verse notes the warning to beware of Alexander, who opposed the gospel. The grammatical voices note the caution. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's warning to Timothy: "of whom be thou ware (phylassou — be on guard against, beware) also" — Timothy too is to be on his guard against Alexander; the enemy who harmed Paul may harm Timothy or the work.

On "he hath greatly withstood our words," the witnesses note the opposition. Barnes and Calvin note the ground of the warning: "he hath greatly (vehemently, exceedingly) withstood (antesteke — opposed, resisted, set himself against) our words (our preaching, our message, the doctrine we taught)" — Alexander's harm was not merely personal but doctrinal; he vigorously opposed the gospel Paul preached.

On "our words," the grammatical-historical note the cause: the opposition was to "our words" — the apostolic message; Alexander resisted the truth, making him a danger to the church as well as to Paul; hence the warning to Timothy. The verse displays the warning to beware of Alexander.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's warning — "of whom be thou ware (be on guard against, beware) also," Timothy to guard against Alexander who may harm the work (Barnes, Robertson); "he hath greatly (vehemently) withstood (opposed) our words (preaching)," his harm doctrinal, vigorously opposing the gospel (Barnes, Calvin); the opposition to "our words," the apostolic message, making him a danger to the church (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the warning. The verse marks the caution: be thou ware. The verse displays the opponent: Alexander. The verse displays the opposition: withstood our words. The verse displays the danger: to the work. The warning displays Timothy put on guard against the gospel's foe. The verse displays the beware: charged. The verse displays the withstanding: vehement. The verse displays the words: opposed. The warning to beware of Alexander, who opposed the gospel, displays Paul's care for Timothy and the work: Timothy too is to be on his guard against Alexander, for he had vehemently withstood the apostolic message — an opponent of the truth dangerous to the church. The verse displays the warning against the foe, set against Paul's lonely first defence (v.16).
16:
"At my first answer no man stood with me, but all men forsook me: I pray God that it may not be laid to their charge." The witnesses note Paul forsaken at his first defence, yet praying for his deserters. On "at my first answer no man stood with me," the grammatical voices note the abandonment. Barnes and Robertson note the lonely scene: "at my first answer (apologia — my first defence, the first hearing of his trial) no man stood with me (no one supported me, appeared as my advocate or friend), but all men forsook (deserted, abandoned) me" — at his first court appearance, every friend deserted him, leaving him to face the tribunal alone.

On "all men forsook me," the witnesses note the desertion. Barnes and Calvin note the completeness: "all men forsook me" — none had the courage to stand by him at his trial, fearing to be associated with one accused before Nero; the loneliness of the forsaken apostle.

On "I pray God that it may not be laid to their charge," the puritan Henry notes the Christlike forgiveness: "I pray God that it may not be laid to their charge (not reckoned against them, forgiven)" — Paul, like Stephen (Acts 7:60) and his Lord (Luke 23:34), prays for the forgiveness of those who deserted him; no resentment, only intercession. The verse displays Paul forsaken at his first defence, yet praying for his deserters.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the lonely scene — "at my first answer (defence, first hearing) no man stood with me, but all men forsook (deserted) me," every friend deserting him at his trial (Barnes, Robertson); the completeness, none daring to stand by one accused before Nero (Barnes, Calvin); "I pray God that it may not be laid to their charge," Paul praying for their forgiveness like Stephen and his Lord, no resentment (Henry). The verse displays the forsaken apostle. The verse marks the abandonment: no man stood with me. The verse displays the desertion: all forsook me. The verse displays the prayer: not laid to their charge. The verse displays the spirit: Christlike forgiveness. The scene displays Paul deserted yet forgiving. The verse displays the defence: alone. The verse displays the forsaking: complete. The verse displays the prayer: for them. Paul forsaken at his first defence, yet praying for his deserters, displays the loneliness and the grace of the dying apostle: at his first trial every friend abandoned him, leaving him to face the tribunal alone — yet he prays, like his Lord, that their desertion not be laid to their charge. The verse displays the forgiving apostle, sustained by the Lord's presence (v.17).
17:
"Notwithstanding the Lord stood with me, and strengthened me; that by me the preaching might be fully known, and that all the Gentiles might hear: and I was delivered out of the mouth of the lion." The witnesses note the Lord standing with Paul when all forsook him. On "the Lord stood with me, and strengthened me," the grammatical voices note the divine presence. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast with the deserting men: "notwithstanding the Lord stood with me (was present, took his stand beside me), and strengthened (empowered, gave inward strength to) me" — when every human friend failed, the Lord himself stood by Paul and gave him strength; the divine Friend more than made up for the absent.

On "that by me the preaching might be fully known, and that all the Gentiles might hear," the witnesses note the purpose. Barnes and Calvin note the aim of his being upheld: "that by me the preaching (the proclamation of the gospel) might be fully known (fully accomplished, made known to the full), and that all the Gentiles might hear" — the Lord strengthened him so that, even at his trial, the gospel might be proclaimed before the highest tribunal and reach the Gentile world.

On "I was delivered out of the mouth of the lion," the grammatical-historical note the rescue: "I was delivered out of the mouth of the lion" — whether Nero, or the immediate danger of death, or Satan; for the present he was rescued from the jaws of destruction. The verse displays the Lord standing with Paul when all forsook him.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the divine presence — "the Lord stood with me (took his stand beside me), and strengthened (empowered) me," the Lord standing by when every friend failed (Barnes, Robertson); the purpose "that by me the preaching might be fully known, and that all the Gentiles might hear," upheld that the gospel be proclaimed before the tribunal and reach the Gentiles (Barnes, Calvin); "delivered out of the mouth of the lion," rescued from death or Nero or Satan (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the Lord's presence. The verse marks the presence: the Lord stood with me. The verse displays the strength: strengthened me. The verse displays the purpose: the gospel fully proclaimed. The verse displays the rescue: from the lion's mouth. The note displays the Lord upholding Paul when men deserted. The verse displays the Lord: present. The verse displays the strengthening: given. The verse displays the deliverance: from the lion. The Lord standing with Paul when all forsook him displays the faithfulness of God in the apostle's darkest hour: when every man deserted him, the Lord himself stood by and strengthened him — that the gospel might be fully proclaimed and all the Gentiles hear — and delivered him out of the lion's mouth. The verse displays the divine presence, the ground of Paul's confidence for the future (v.18).
18:
"And the Lord shall deliver me from every evil work, and will preserve me unto his heavenly kingdom: to whom be glory for ever and ever. Amen." The witnesses note Paul's confidence of final deliverance into the heavenly kingdom. On "the Lord shall deliver me from every evil work," the grammatical voices note the assurance. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's confident hope, rising from past deliverance (v.17): "the Lord shall deliver (rescue) me from every evil work (every evil deed, every attempt of his enemies, or every assault that would harm his soul)" — not necessarily from death, but from every evil that would do him real (spiritual) harm; the Lord will keep him to the end.

On "will preserve me unto his heavenly kingdom," the witnesses note the goal. Barnes and Calvin note the hope: "will preserve (save, bring safely) me unto (into) his heavenly kingdom" — the Lord will bring him safely through death into the kingdom of heaven; deliverance now crowned by salvation into glory; death itself the gateway to the kingdom.

On "to whom be glory for ever and ever. Amen," the puritan Henry notes the doxology: the thought of such deliverance and glory bursts into praise — "to whom be glory for ever and ever. Amen" — ascribing everlasting glory to the Lord (Christ, or God) who saves him. The verse displays Paul's confidence of final deliverance into the heavenly kingdom.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's confident hope — "the Lord shall deliver (rescue) me from every evil work," not from death but from every evil that would harm his soul, keeping him to the end (Barnes, Robertson); "will preserve (bring safely) me unto his heavenly kingdom," bringing him through death into the kingdom of glory (Barnes, Calvin); "to whom be glory for ever and ever. Amen," bursting into a doxology of everlasting praise (Henry). The verse displays the confidence of glory. The verse marks the deliverance: from every evil work. The verse displays the preservation: unto the heavenly kingdom. The verse displays the goal: glory. The verse displays the doxology: glory for ever. The confidence displays Paul sure of being brought safely to glory. The verse displays the deliverance: assured. The verse displays the kingdom: heavenly. The verse displays the glory: ascribed. Paul's confidence of final deliverance into the heavenly kingdom displays the serene hope that crowns his last words about himself: the Lord will deliver him from every evil that would harm his soul and bring him safely into his heavenly kingdom — to whom he ascribes everlasting glory. The verse displays the apostle's doxology, before his closing greetings (vv.19-21).
19:
"Salute Prisca and Aquila, and the household of Onesiphorus." The brief verse notes the greeting to Prisca, Aquila, and the household of Onesiphorus. The grammatical voices note the greetings. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's closing salutations: "salute (greet) Prisca (Priscilla) and Aquila" — the devoted husband-and-wife team, Paul's old fellow-workers and friends (Acts 18:2-3; Romans 16:3), tentmakers like himself, who had risked their lives for him.

On "Prisca and Aquila," the witnesses note their faithfulness. Barnes and Ellicott note that this couple appears repeatedly in the New Testament as devoted, hospitable believers who hosted a house-church and instructed Apollos (Acts 18:26); their long fidelity is honoured in this final greeting.

On "the household of Onesiphorus," the grammatical-historical note the faithful family: "the household (family) of Onesiphorus" — the family of the one who had so faithfully refreshed Paul and sought him out at Rome (1:16-18); Paul greets the household of his loyal friend. The verse displays the greeting to Prisca, Aquila, and the household of Onesiphorus.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's salutations — "salute (greet) Prisca (Priscilla) and Aquila," his old fellow-workers and friends, tentmakers who risked their lives for him (Acts 18, Barnes, Robertson); the couple devoted, hospitable believers who hosted a house-church and instructed Apollos (Barnes, Ellicott); "the household of Onesiphorus," the family of his loyal friend who refreshed and sought him out (1:16-18, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the greetings. The verse marks the first: Prisca and Aquila. The verse displays their record: faithful fellow-workers. The verse displays the second: Onesiphorus's household. The verse displays the loyalty: refreshed Paul. The greetings display Paul saluting his faithful friends. The verse displays the Prisca: greeted. The verse displays the Aquila: greeted. The verse displays the household: of Onesiphorus. The greeting to Prisca, Aquila, and the household of Onesiphorus displays the warmth of Paul's closing salutations: he greets his devoted fellow-workers Prisca and Aquila, and the household of Onesiphorus — his loyal friend who had refreshed him and sought him out at Rome. The verse displays the faithful friends, amid news of others (v.20).
20:
"Erastus abode at Corinth: but Trophimus have I left at Miletum sick." The brief verse notes the news of Erastus at Corinth and Trophimus left sick. The grammatical voices note the report. Barnes and Robertson note the personal news Paul sends: "Erastus (perhaps the city treasurer of Corinth, Romans 16:23, or the companion of Acts 19:22) abode (remained, stayed) at Corinth" — Erastus had stayed behind at Corinth, not come on to Rome.

On "Trophimus have I left at Miletum sick," the witnesses note the sick companion. Barnes and Calvin note the touching detail: "Trophimus (the Ephesian, Acts 20:4; 21:29) have I left at Miletum (Miletus) sick" — Paul had been obliged to leave his companion Trophimus behind, ill, at Miletus; the apostle who had healed others could not, or did not, heal his own friend.

On the detail, the grammatical-historical note its bearing: the fact that Paul left Trophimus sick — not miraculously healing him — shows that the gift of healing was exercised at God's will, not at the apostle's own; miracles served the gospel, not personal convenience. The verse displays the news of Erastus at Corinth and Trophimus left sick.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the personal news — "Erastus (the Corinthian, perhaps the treasurer) abode (stayed) at Corinth," remaining behind not coming to Rome (Barnes, Robertson); "Trophimus (the Ephesian) have I left at Miletum sick," obliged to leave his ill companion behind (Acts 20-21, Barnes, Calvin); the fact showing the gift of healing exercised at God's will not the apostle's (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the news. The verse marks the Erastus: at Corinth. The verse displays the Trophimus: left sick. The verse displays the place: Miletus. The verse displays the bearing: healing at God's will. The report displays Paul's companions' whereabouts. The verse displays the Erastus: stayed. The verse displays the Trophimus: ill. The verse displays the leaving: behind. The news of Erastus at Corinth and Trophimus left sick displays the personal items Paul shares with Timothy: Erastus had remained at Corinth, and Trophimus, fallen ill, Paul had been obliged to leave behind at Miletus — a reminder that even the apostle's healing gift was exercised only at God's will. The verse displays the scattered companions, with an urgent plea to come (v.21).
21:
"Do thy diligence to come before winter. Eubulus greeteth thee, and Pudens, and Linus, and Claudia, and all the brethren." The witnesses note the urgent plea to come before winter, with greetings from the Roman brethren. On "do thy diligence to come before winter," the grammatical voices note the urgency. Barnes and Robertson note Paul renews his plea (v.9) with a pressing reason: "do thy diligence (make haste, do your best) to come before winter" — before the season when sea travel became dangerous or impossible (navigation closed in winter); if Timothy delayed, he might not be able to come, or might come too late — Paul's death being near.

On "before winter," the witnesses note the double urgency. Barnes and Henry note that "before winter" carries both the practical reason (travel) and the unspoken one (Paul's approaching death); the cloak, too (v.13), was wanted against the winter cold; the plea is tender and pressing.

On the greetings, the grammatical-historical note the Roman believers: "Eubulus greeteth thee, and Pudens, and Linus, and Claudia, and all the brethren" — the few faithful believers at Rome who remained with Paul (Linus by tradition later bishop of Rome) send their greetings; the little band around the imprisoned apostle. The verse displays the urgent plea to come before winter, with greetings.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the urgency — "do thy diligence (make haste) to come before winter," before sea travel closed, lest Timothy come too late (Barnes, Robertson); "before winter" carrying both the practical reason and Paul's approaching death, the plea tender and pressing (Barnes, Henry); the greetings of "Eubulus, Pudens, Linus, Claudia, and all the brethren," the few faithful at Rome (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the plea and greetings. The verse marks the urgency: before winter. The verse displays the reason: travel and death. The verse displays the greeters: the Roman brethren. The verse displays the band: faithful few. The plea displays Paul urging Timothy to come in time. The verse displays the diligence: urged. The verse displays the winter: the deadline. The verse displays the brethren: greeting. The urgent plea to come before winter, with greetings from the Roman brethren, displays the pressing tenderness of the apostle's last request: Timothy is to make haste and come before winter closes the sea — lest he come too late — and the few faithful believers at Rome send their greetings. The verse displays the final plea and salutations, sealed by the closing benediction (v.22).
22:
"The Lord Jesus Christ be with thy spirit. Grace be with you. Amen." The witnesses note the closing benediction upon Timothy and the church. On "the Lord Jesus Christ be with thy spirit," the grammatical voices note the personal blessing. Barnes and Robertson note the tender final word to Timothy: "the Lord Jesus Christ be with thy spirit" — Paul's parting prayer for his beloved son, that the presence of the Lord Jesus would be with Timothy's inmost soul; the personal, intimate blessing.

On "thy spirit," the witnesses note the inwardness. Barnes and Gill note that the blessing is upon "thy spirit" — the inner man, the soul; Paul prays the Lord's presence into the very heart of Timothy, the deepest place of his being.

On "grace be with you. Amen," the grammatical-historical note the wider benediction: turning from Timothy to the whole church ("you," plural), Paul closes with his customary "grace be with you" — the grace of the Lord Jesus upon all the believers, sealed with "Amen." These are likely the last written words of the apostle. The verse displays the closing benediction upon Timothy and the church.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the personal blessing — "the Lord Jesus Christ be with thy spirit," Paul's parting prayer that the Lord's presence be with Timothy's inmost soul (Barnes, Robertson); "thy spirit," the inner man, the Lord's presence prayed into Timothy's heart (Barnes, Gill); "grace be with you (plural). Amen," turning to the whole church with his customary benediction, likely his last written words (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the benediction. The verse marks the blessing: the Lord with thy spirit. The verse displays the inwardness: thy spirit. The verse displays the breadth: grace be with you. The verse displays the close: likely his last words. The benediction displays Paul blessing Timothy and the church. The verse displays the Lord: with thy spirit. The verse displays the grace: with you. The verse displays the Amen: the seal. The closing benediction upon Timothy and the church displays the tender seal of the apostle's final letter: he prays the presence of the Lord Jesus Christ upon Timothy's spirit, and grace upon the whole church — words that, fittingly, may be the last the apostle ever wrote. The verse displays the grace of Christ that closes the second epistle to Timothy and Paul's written ministry.
